glossary.definition: A field of study focused on creating intelligent systems.
glossary.sentence: Artificial Intelligence: a Guide to Intelligent Systems.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence standard used in some definitions of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: An approach that underlies systems that think humanly, considering human beings as information processing systems.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: An entity capable of perceiving its environment and acting within it to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence by determining if a tester can differentiate between answers given by a human and those given by an artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: A capability required to pass the Turing test for maintaining successful communication with a tester as a human being does.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does

glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems, defined as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A capability required to pass the Turing test in order to use stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : ... automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions

glossary.definition: A branch of artificial intelligence and a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building, based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns, and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building.

glossary.definition: A test that requires a computer to possess computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems that considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour, whereas connectionist artificial intelligence attributes this to the discovery of relationships in data (with little or no prior knowledge) (Goel, 2021) .

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain, consisting of a large number of interconnected neurons, where knowledge is considered a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A hypothesis that underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing.
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A system that includes symbols, symbolic structures, and operations, capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A criticism of the physical symbol system hypothesis describing a situation where a system correctly manipulates physical symbols without having actual intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: Systems that can be applied to a diversity of tasks including classification, pattern recognition, prediction, optimization, noise filtering, and non-linear estimation.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal; non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: Also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, it is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is concerned with developing solutions for specific tasks.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: Also known as strong artificial intelligence or human-level intelligence, it treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied or replicated, allowing a system to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, as well as a human.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: An approach that focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales, asserting that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence .

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but instead use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, and automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods based on a great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: A way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world.

glossary.definition: Places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.

glossary.definition: Ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: A representation that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects; also called a formal specification.
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3). The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: The state of a problem-solving process at the present moment, such as a blank grid at the start of a game.
glossary.sentence: First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: The state of a problem-solving process that a player or system aims to reach.
glossary.sentence: Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: A representation of a game consisting of states, including an initial state and goal states.
glossary.sentence: To implement a game, it is not sufficient to represent the game using a state space graph.

glossary.definition: Elements in a state space graph corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes (the description given below is partly based on the information given in (Luger, 2009)) : nodes corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process and has no ancestors.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one root node that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have ancestors;

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process and has no descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: Nodes in a state space graph that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process and have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: Elements in a state space graph that represent transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process.

glossary.term: Zero-sum deterministic two-person games with perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where two players compete, make alternate moves, have complete information about the game course, involve no randomness, and one player's loss is equal to another's gain.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information.

glossary.definition: Games where there is no level of randomness involved.
glossary.sentence: based on the level of randomness associated with a game - deterministic vs. stochastic games, and others.

glossary.definition: Games that involve a level of randomness, such as the use of a die.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.definition: A condition in a game where both players are aware of all information about the course of the game, including past moves, future moves, and available objects.
glossary.sentence: based on the availability of information about the course of the game for both players - perfect information vs. imperfect information

glossary.definition: A condition in a game where part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: A process that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, an intelligent computer system needs a search algorithm that processes the data structure to find a solution to the problem based on known input data (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A known node in the state space graph used for the search purpose.
glossary.sentence: The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose.

glossary.definition: A known node in the state space graph used for the search purpose.
glossary.sentence: The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose.

glossary.definition: A path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node, corresponding to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: A solution path is a path through the state space graph from a given search start node to a given search goal node.

glossary.definition: Nodes that have been generated by the search algorithm, but whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A search method, also known as forward chaining, that begins with a problem description or given data and matches it against the IF part of production rules.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): data-driven search (forward chaining ); goal-driven search (backward chaining).

glossary.definition: A search method, also known as backward chaining, where a goal is added to the working memory and matched against the THEN part of production rules to find evidence.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph by returning to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants or ancestors if the current path does not reach the goal.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms cannot distinguish between paths that allow them to reach the search goal node and paths that do not.

glossary.definition: Another name for uninformed search, also called blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that use the state space graph plus additional information about node quality to distinguish between paths.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms use not only the state space graph but also additional information about node quality.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list containing search nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have already been expanded.
glossary.sentence: The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: A search method where the algorithm starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A data structure that works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle, where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A node that does not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm tries expanding the current node J, but this node does not have descendants as it is a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A situation in a state space graph with many node levels where a search algorithm spends a long time searching in depth before returning to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes the problem of going along indefinitely deep paths but may fail to find a solution if the goal node is located below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: Values added to nodes that allow the algorithm to determine if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores nodes level-by-level, starting from closer branches before moving to the ends of branches.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the First in, First Out principle, where the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search that performs a search simultaneously in both directions—data-driven and goal-driven—until a point of contact is found.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge used to solve problems, often based on experience, that depends on the nature of the problem.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: A function used to acquire heuristic measures for terminal nodes in a game.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: Numerical values assigned to nodes of the state space graph that represent the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A problem occurring when a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead fails to detect a heuristically promising path, potentially leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph, without implementing backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node determined by heuristic measures, but can suffer from shortcomings like the local maximum problem or the plateau problem.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration while keeping all previously found nodes, allowing it to switch between different paths in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list, working according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that acts as a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search by selecting a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: A defined parameter used to select a specific number of best nodes based on heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on the heuristic measures.

glossary.definition: A graph that includes all states from the initial state of the game to all goal states.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A graph where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have the measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A player whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the minimiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be smaller, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned to the root node in a complete state space graph, indicating the outcome if both players play rationally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node where all nodes are closely related and have heuristic measures equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that divides a graph into levels, assigns heuristic measures to leaf nodes, and propagates them up to the root node to determine the optimal game result.
glossary.sentence: To apply the minimax algorithm, we divide the graph into levels, starting with either the MAX or MIN level.

glossary.definition: An alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves search efficiency by cutting off unpromising paths from the state space graph that are not beneficial to the final decision.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning is an alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves the search efficiency of the state space graph of the game.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MAX levels that defines the best move to maximize the game result and should not decrease.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MIN levels that defines the best move to minimize the game result and should not increase.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels.

glossary.definition: A process performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A process performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node that indicates the potential outcome of a game, which can be precise or intermediate.
glossary.sentence: When a precise heuristic measure is assigned, the algorithm goes one level up, via the same path that was used to go down.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the ancestor node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1, and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: An approach used to process a partial state space graph that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores a partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: The study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: A set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task, explicitly programmed by developers where each step is coded in a precise way.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task.

glossary.definition: A particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, also called a data point, an observation, or an example.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: A collection of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: A measurable characteristic or property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, also called an attribute.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: A specific value of a feature.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: A set of all features characterising a data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: An external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before training, also called a model hyperparameter.
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation of the relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: A result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: An internal configuration variable of a model whose value is found or learnt by a machine learning algorithm during training, also called a model parameter.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: The process of checking the performance of a model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: The process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: A step typically including replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.
glossary.sentence: Data pre-processing (Step 4) typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.

glossary.definition: A category of machine learning where the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.
glossary.sentence: The first topic of this module explained that, in supervised learning, the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.

glossary.definition: Deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs.
glossary.sentence: classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs

glossary.definition: A task in supervised learning where the output is a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object; it also refers to searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between an independent and a dependent variable.
glossary.sentence: In general, the concept of regression means searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: independent one and dependent one.

glossary.definition: A type of learning based on unlabelled data, also called learning without a teacher, with the goal to reveal patterns or structure of data.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher.

glossary.definition: A process of dividing an available dataset into subsets that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: An unsupervised learning task that finds rules based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised learning task associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A reward-based learning suitable for agents who operate in a changing environment and need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning.

glossary.definition: A component of a supervised machine learning algorithm that measures how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples;

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression that assumes a linear relationship between input variables and a single output variable using a linear function.
glossary.sentence: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression.

glossary.definition: A real number corresponding to the intercept, which is the point where the line crosses the y-axis or the value of y when x is equal to 0.
glossary.sentence: It is also usually called a bias term/coefficient .

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression that has a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010):

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features.
glossary.sentence: multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model

glossary.definition: A loss function used by the linear regression model, also known as L2 loss.
glossary.sentence: The linear regression model uses a loss function called squared loss (also known as L2 loss) (Google Developers, n.d.; Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A metric used to evaluate the performance of an algorithm by accumulating the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values, based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables to minimise the cost function.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: A positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1 that determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.
glossary.sentence: A learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks. It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: A classification task where there are only two possible classes or categories represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ).

glossary.definition: A classification task where there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm used for classification tasks that predicts one of two classes by applying a sigmoid function to the linear function's value to squash it within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task.

glossary.definition: A function applied to the value of a linear function to squash it within the range of [0,1], with a threshold value of 0.5 used to decide the class of a data object.
glossary.sentence: Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks by identifying the most similar neighbours in the existing dataset based on a similarity metric.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A geometric distance metric used to calculate similarity for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A geometric distance metric used to calculate similarity for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A distance metric for categorical feature values that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A version of the nearest neighbour algorithm that classifies a new data object by finding the single closest data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that uses a hyperparameter k to define the number of nearest data objects considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used.

glossary.definition: A value set by the developer before the operation of the algorithm that defines the number of nearest data objects to be considered.
glossary.sentence: It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning that uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.
glossary.sentence: In the first topic of Module 4, you learnt that unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: Subsets of data made of one or more data objects, characterized by the similarity of data objects within the cluster and the difference of data objects between the clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm based on the concepts of distance and a centroid.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: The average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that maps each data object to only one cluster, aiming to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.
glossary.sentence: The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.

glossary.definition: The hyperparameter of the K-Means algorithm representing the number of clusters that need to be generated.
glossary.sentence: Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that builds a hierarchy of clusters where clusters at each level are created by merging or dividing clusters at the next lower level.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is another unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters as the K-Means algorithm does.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters where each data object is initially attributed to its own cluster and then recursively merged.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits existing clusters into two new clusters.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: Methods used to measure the similarity or distance between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between the most distant elements of two clusters is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between the closest elements of two clusters is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset, serving as an output of hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering.

glossary.definition: A metric that measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric that applies a square root to MSE and provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”.

glossary.definition: The average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination, it indicates how close the model built fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: A correct prediction where a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: A correct prediction where a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: An erroneous prediction where a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not.

glossary.definition: An erroneous prediction where a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam.

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category, representing detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) .

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives, representing the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.definition: A metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: The mean of recall values calculated for all classes in a multiclass classification task.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: The phenomenon where increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff.

glossary.definition: A metric representing the harmonic mean of precision and recall used in classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A visualization of a classifier's behavior under different parameter values by plotting false positive rate versus true positive rate.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A metric that provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve by measuring the entire two-dimensional area underneath it.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning task that demands dividing data objects into representative groups.
glossary.sentence: In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups.

glossary.definition: A metric for clustering performance that represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.

glossary.definition: A plot representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain consisting of a cell body (soma), dendrites for receiving signals, and an axon for transmitting signals.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron, serving as the site for signal transmission.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron that receives input signals, combines them using a specific function, and uses an activation function to determine an output signal.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Real-valued parameters that express the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output, serving as the basic means of long-term memory.
glossary.sentence: Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals by summing up the weighted values of inputs.
glossary.sentence: It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs.

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships;

glossary.definition: An input typically indexed with 0 that provides the offset of the line or hyperplane from the origin.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: A system comprising one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern that can perform complicated tasks through parallel processing.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The individual neurons within an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: The layers of neurons located between the input and output layers of an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that has connections only in one direction with no loops, where every processing unit receives input from upstream units and delivers output to downstream units.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: The property where an artificial neural network's operation changes very little despite partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: Identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data

glossary.definition: Identifying a disease from symptoms or causes from effects.
glossary.sentence: prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects

glossary.definition: Finding the best organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints

glossary.definition: Separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal.
glossary.sentence: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal

glossary.definition: Determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: A model developed by Rosenblatt in the late 1950s.
glossary.sentence: Perceptron Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The simplest and commonly known type of artificial neural network that can learn only linearly separable functions, consisting of an input layer and an output layer.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: One cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network that includes an input layer, at least one hidden layer, and an output layer.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network

glossary.definition: Propagation of error backwards in the network from the output layer to the previous hidden layer and so on.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer

glossary.definition: An algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components: the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A component that maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations, storing a representation of both facts and relations among them.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.

glossary.definition: A component that relates production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows methods and tools of artificial intelligence and has knowledge of how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A person who has deep knowledge of both facts and rules and substantial practical experience in a particular, usually limited, domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An automated system used by the domain expert or knowledge engineer to describe knowledge, build a knowledge base, and analyze or generate knowledge.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Computer systems that emulate the decision-making ability of a human expert.
glossary.sentence: Understanding decision support systems and expert systems.

glossary.definition: Determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: Finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: Devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: Comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: Governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation method using graphs where each node describes a complex structure of an object or concept, including its properties and parts, utilizing a referencing principle for relations.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: A feature of frame-based expert systems where sub-class and instance frames acquire slots and values from parent class frames.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A structure that stores knowledge about each object in one place, provides hierarchical organization, supports inheritance, and allows adding executable demons to slots.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: A property of an object or concept within a frame, which can hold symbolic, numerical, or boolean values, or reference another frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure that provides extended knowledge about slot values, controls access, and tells the inference engine how to process the property.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A frame containing common properties and default values for a set, class, or type of objects, used to represent problems in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: A frame representing detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain, typically derived from class or sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A relationship that links a sub-class frame to a class frame or an instance frame to a sub-class/class frame, enabling hierarchical structures.
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.

glossary.definition: A relationship that associates frames corresponding to the components of an object with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: A relation that allows defining any other type of connection between frames using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: The assignment of methods or procedures to slots, such as regular procedures or demons, to perform calculations or actions.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure invoked automatically as a side effect of accessing or manipulating a slot value.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A mechanism that can calculate values or perform tasks when requested.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem, presented in the form IF condition THEN consequence.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A component that keeps track of the current state of the problem-solving process by storing facts, conclusions, and other case-specific data.
glossary.sentence: The rule-based expert system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process contained in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A control cycle where production rules stored in the knowledge base are matched against a pattern.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 explains the operational principles of the control cycle “Recognize-act”.

glossary.definition: A technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A method where an expert system has a goal and attempts to find the evidence to prove it, corresponding to a decomposition of the goal into simpler subgoals.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Computer-based systems that help decision makers utilize data and models to solve unstructured problems.
glossary.sentence: Understanding decision support systems and expert systems.

2. Define them using ONLY the provided text.
3. NO external knowledge. Synthesize exclusively from source.
4. DO NOT translate. Keep definitions in the source language.
5. If no terms found, return an empty list.
